narration.default: In Scratch, the "if-then-else" block was a C-shaped block with TWO slots inside — one for "then do this" and one for "else do this". A hexagon-shaped condition slotted into the top. C has the exact same idea, just typed differently: if (...) { ... } else { ... }. The hexagon condition becomes round PARENTHESES. The two SUBSTACK slots become two pairs of CURLY BRACES. The logic is identical — based on a true/false test, run one of two paths. Today you'll write your first if-else in C, then test it with different values to see both branches fire.

Line 4 is just int age = 12; — a regular int variable holding 12. The next lines will check this variable against the threshold for being a teenager (13). The whole point of if-else is to make different things happen based on the VALUE of a variable — without if-else your program would do exactly the same thing every time, no matter what.

Lines 5 to 7 are the if block: if (age >= 13) { printf("You are a teenager."); }. Three pieces. (1) The keyword if. (2) The CONDITION inside parentheses: age >= 13 means "age greater than or equal to 13". (3) The BODY inside curly braces: the code that runs when the condition is true. The braces { } work the same way as Scratch's SUBSTACK slot — they hold "the stuff to do when the condition matches". You can put as many statements inside the braces as you want.

Lines 7 to 9 are the else block: } else { printf("Not a teenager yet."); }. The else block runs when the if condition is FALSE. EXACTLY ONE of the two branches will run — never both, never neither. This is the key superpower of if-else: it picks one path. Without an else, you only have an "if" that may or may not run; with else, you guarantee that one branch always runs. Use else when you want a fallback for "what to do if the condition wasn't met".

Before clicking Run, predict in your head which branch fires. age is 12. The condition is age >= 13. Is 12 greater than or equal to 13? NO. So the if condition is FALSE, and the else branch runs. Click Run. The Console shows "Not a teenager yet." Now change age to 15 and Run again. This time 15 >= 13 is TRUE, so the if branch wins and the Console shows "You are a teenager." Try age = 13 too (right at the boundary — the >= includes 13).

Here are all the C comparison operators you'll use in conditions: < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal), == (EQUAL — note TWO equals signs), != (NOT equal). The double-equals == catches everyone at least once. SINGLE equals = means assignment ("set this variable to that value"); DOUBLE equals == means comparison ("are these two values the same?"). Mixing them up is the most common bug in all of C. If you write if (age = 13), C will SET age to 13 and the if always runs (because 13 is treated as "true"). You almost certainly meant if (age == 13). Triple-check this every single time.
